reasoning:
The Federal Reserve's MBS holdings naturally run off by roughly $12-$15 billion per month as part of their Quantitative Tightening (QT) program. The Fed has repeatedly stated their intention to hold an all-Treasury portfolio in the long run; thus, even as they taper Treasury runoff, they maintain the MBS runoff cap of $35 billion (and reinvest any excess into Treasuries, not MBS). Because prepayments are slow, the MBS balance shrinks steadily every month without hitting the cap.

Historically, this principal runoff hits the Fed's weekly balance sheet (H.4.1) on the first Wednesday on or after the 25th of the month (aligned with Fannie Mae's schedule). For June 2025, the 25th is exactly a Wednesday. Therefore, a massive ~$13 billion drop will hit the balance sheet on June 25, sending the MBS balance far below the June 18 baseline of 2,156,168 million. This effectively guarantees a decrease by the June 29 resolution date.

For the July, September, and December resolution dates, the balance will continue stepping down by ~$13B each month. The only catalyst that could drive the balance back above the June 2025 baseline would be an abrupt policy reversal to begin large-scale Quantitative Easing (QE) specifically targeting MBS purchases. Short of a severe financial crisis manifesting in the second half of 2025, there is virtually no reason for the Fed to do this. Following instructions, the probabilities are floored near the 0.05 lower bound and increase slightly into the future to account for the tail risk of a crisis.
